perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The Parties irrevocably agree that the Courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the jurisdiction clause to be incomplete omits the selection of courts, thus impacting the enforceability of the agreement in a dispute.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the clause specifying the Delaware courts as having exclusive jurisdiction, it creates ambiguity. If the parties are diverse (i.e., citizens of different states) and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, the case could potentially be heard in federal court due to diversity jurisdiction, contradicting the implied intent to have the dispute settled in Delaware state courts alone, which is undermined by removing the 'exclusive jurisdiction' clause. 28 U.S.C. § 1332 defines the scope of diversity jurisdiction.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text omits the clause specifying that Delaware courts have exclusive jurisdiction, which creates a potential legal contradiction. According to the Law Explanation, if the parties are diverse and the amount exceeds $75,000, the case could be heard in federal court. This contradicts the intent to have disputes settled solely in Delaware state courts. This contradiction is supported by 28 U.S.C. § 1332, which both snippets describe, stating that district courts have jurisdiction in civil actions exceeding $75,000 between citizens of different states. By removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause, the contract opens the door for federal jurisdiction, undermining the original agreement's implied intention.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) ari sing from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase that includes 'any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group', the power to seek compensation for damage is diminished.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-715. Incidental and Consequential Damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the reference to consequential damages within the indemnification clause, the agreement contradicts the principles outlined in UCC § 2-715 regarding a seller's incidental and consequential damages arising from a buyer's breach. While this NDA isn't a sale of goods, indemnity provisions often contemplate potential consequential damages. Excluding specific mention undermines the possibility of recovering such losses if a breach occurs, thereby limiting remedies available under general contract law principles reflected in the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-715. Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-715. Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. (1) Incidental damages resulting from the seller's breach include expenses reasonably incurred in inspection, receipt , transportation and care and custody of goods rightfully rejected, any commercially reasonable charges, expenses or commissions in connection with effecting cover and any other reasonable expense incident to the delay or other breach. (2) Consequential damages resulting from the seller's breach include (a) any loss resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know and which could not reasonably be prevented by cover or otherwise; and (b) injury to person or property proximately resulting from any breach of warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The original contract text included 'any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group'. The changed text removes this phrase. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the possibility of recovering such losses if a breach occurs, thereby limiting remedies available under general contract law principles reflected in the UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-715, discusses 'Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages', defining consequential damages as 'any loss resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know'. By removing the explicit mention of consequential damages, the modified contract weakens the ability of the Disclosing Party to be fully indemnified for potential losses stemming from a breach, creating a legal contradiction. This falls under the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction because the removal of specific damage types affects the clarity and enforceability of the indemnification clause.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement's termination from 2 years to indefinite. An agreement lasting in perpetuity can potentially be considered unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 204. Supplying an Essential Term
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the termination clause to 'indefinitely' can be viewed as an unconscionable term. Courts look to the reasonableness of contract terms. An NDA with no end date imposes a potentially unlimited restriction on information use, which may be considered unfair, particularly if it hinders a party's ability to compete or innovate. This violates Section 204 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts which deals with 'Supplying an Essential Term' - and implies that the court must set the duration of the contract instead of a contractually agreed term.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 2-year termination, while the changed text makes the agreement indefinite. The Law Explanation indicates that indefinite terms in an NDA can be viewed as unconscionable, potentially hindering a party's ability to compete or innovate, and thus contradicting contract law principles. The Law Explanation invokes Section 204 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, suggesting that a court might need to set the duration of the contract instead of the parties agreeing on a term (because the indefinite term is unconscionable). This supports the determination that a contradiction exists because the changed text may violate the principle of reasonable contract terms.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication required to be given under this Agreement shall not be v alidly given if sent by e- mail.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication required to be given under this Agreement shall be validly given if sent by e-mail.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the method of valid contact communication between parties in the agreement from in-person, mail or commercial courier to email can open an attack vector for phishing scams and other forms of deception.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (ESIGN), 15 U.S.C. § 7001
perturbation.law_explanation: While ESIGN generally validates electronic signatures and communications, the alteration to allow email as a valid form of notice, without additional security measures, can increase the risk of disputes over authenticity and receipt. ESIGN requires demonstrable consent to electronic communication, and simply stating that email is valid might not satisfy that requirement, particularly if it deviates from standard business practices or increases risk for a party that is not technologically sophisticated, which is a form of imposing consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 15 - COMMERCE AND TRADE 15 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 15 - COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 96 - ELECTRONIC SIGNATURES IN GLOBAL AND NATIONAL COMMERCE From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov CHAPTER 96—ELECTRONIC SIGNATURES IN GLOBAL AND NATIONAL COMMERCE SUBCHAPTER I—ELECTRONIC RECORDS AND SIGNATURES IN COMMERCE Sec. 7001. General rule of validity. 7002. Exemption to preemption. 7003. Specific exceptions. 7004. Applicability to Federal and State governments. 7005. Studies. 7006. Definitions. SUBCHAPTER II—TRANSFERABLE RECORDS 7021. Transferable records. SUBCHAPTER III—PROMOTION OF INTERNATIONAL ELECTRONIC COMMERCE 7031. Principles governing the use of electronic signatures in international transactions. SUBCHAPTER I—ELECTRONIC RECORDS AND SIGNATURES IN COMMERCE §7001. General rule of validity (a) In general Notwithstanding any statute, regulation, or other rule of law (other than this subchapter and subchapter II of this chapter), with respect to any transaction in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce— (1) a signature, contract, or other record relating to such transaction may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because it is in electronic form; and (2) a contract relating to such transaction may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because an electronic signature or electronic record was used in its formation. (b) Preservation of rights and obligations This subchapter does not— (1) limit, alter, or otherwise affect any requirement imposed by a statute, regulation, or rule of law relating to the rights and obligations of persons under such statute, regulation, or rule of law other than a requirement that contracts or other records be written, signed, or in nonelectronic form; or (2) require any person to agree to use or accept electronic records or electronic signatures, other than a governmental agency with respect to a record other than a contract to which it is a party. (c) Consumer disclosures (1) Consent to electronic records Notwithstanding subsection (a) of this section, if a statute, regulation, or other rule of law requires that information relating to a transaction or transactions in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce be provided or made available to a consumer in writing, the use of an electronic record
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts ESIGN by potentially circumventing the requirement for demonstrable consent to electronic communication. The original text prohibited email, which inherently avoided any issues of consent under ESIGN. The changed text now allows email, but without any stipulations for obtaining or verifying consent, creating a risk that parties may be bound by electronic communications without proper agreement. According to the Law Explanation, simply stating that email is valid might not satisfy ESIGN's consent requirement, especially if it increases risk for a party that is not technologically sophisticated. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section 7001 (c)(1), highlights the necessity of consent to electronic records when a statute requires information to be provided in writing, and the modified text lacks any mechanism to ensure such consent is obtained. This creates a legal contradiction as it introduces a method of communication that may not meet the legal standard for valid electronic communication.
